lesson.menu_title: Block 3 -- The Final Terms: Pushing Back and Reaching the Wording
lesson.menu_desc: You read the agreement -- now negotiate it. Push back on a clause in good faith, redline what needs to change, and propose the exact wording with the formal subjunctive -- "I propose that the clause be amended". Reach the final wording on the last sticking point -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Block 3 -- The Final Terms: Negotiating the Wording
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 19 -- The Final Terms

listenings.text: Carlos, you read the agreement and you flagged the parts that matter. Now we negotiate the final terms before we sign. Let me brief you on how a senior executive pushes back without breaking the deal. First, negotiate in good faith. Good faith means you are honest and you really intend to reach an agreement, not just win. Your counterpart, the person on the other side of the table, reads good faith the same way you do, so stay constructive. Second, name the sticking point. A sticking point is the one issue that is blocking the deal, the part where you cannot yet agree, and you solve it faster when you name it clearly. Third, redline the wording. To redline is to mark the exact change you want in the contract, and the wording is the precise language the clause uses. Small changes in wording protect you, so be specific. Fourth, propose the change formally. In contract talk we use a special form: I propose that the clause be amended, I recommend that the deadline be moved, we ask that the wording remain neutral. Notice the verb stays in its base form, be amended, be moved, remain, after propose, recommend, and ask. To amend is to make a formal change to the wording. Fifth, look for leeway and a carve-out. Leeway is the room to move, the flexibility you have, and a carve-out is a small exception written into a clause so it does not apply in one case. When the wording is too strict, ask for a carve-out instead of rejecting the whole clause. So push back in good faith, name the sticking point, redline the wording, propose the amendment formally, and use leeway and a carve-out to close the gap. That is how a senior executive reaches the final wording.

Hi Carlos, it is Diana, calling before your final negotiation on the agreement. I read your redline, and I want to align on what to push on so you reach the final wording today. First, the good news. Your counterpart is negotiating in good faith, so this is a constructive conversation, not a fight. Keep it that way. Second, the real sticking point. It is the waiver clause in Section nine. The wording is too broad, so do not reject the whole clause. Instead, ask for a carve-out, one narrow exception, and propose it formally: I recommend that the clause be amended to exclude our renegotiation right. That keeps the deal alive and protects us. Third, the closing date. There is some leeway there, more room than the other side admits, so use it. I suggest that the deadline be moved by two weeks, and hold firm. Fourth, the wording on liability. It is fine as drafted, so do not reopen it, you already won that one. Concentrate your push on the carve-out and the date. Redline only what matters, propose each change in good faith, and reach the final wording. You are one negotiation away from signing, Carlos. Strong work. Call me when it is done.
